interactions.summary: A student asks whether gods are also authorities in the first worldview; Jiang answers that polytheistic gods are authorities but not benevolent ones, and that recognizing arbitrary fate can inspire eudaimonia rather than passive pleasure.
Two brief 'Okay?' fragments are diarized as unknown and do not affect the argument.

speaker_notes.note: SPEAKER_03 asks a substantive student question about whether God counts as authority.

claims.claim: In Jiang's contrast, ancient gods are authorities but not benevolent authorities; they are angry, proud, vengeful, and able to get away with what humans cannot.
claims.temporal_scope: polytheistic worldview as explained in lecture

claims.claim: The idea that rulers are benevolent authority figures is described as a new modern concept; older kings rule because the gods favor them, not because they are good.
Polytheistic fate teaches that because tomorrow may destroy you, the point of life is eudaimonia: flourishing and living to the best of one's ability today.
Jiang reverses the apparent agency story: the worldview that says humans lack control inspires excellence, while the worldview that says humans control everything enslaves them.

signature_moments.moment: Because fate can kill you tomorrow, the right answer is not despair but flourishing today.
signature_moments.source_phrase: live your life to the best of your ability today
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is Jiang's strongest positive counter-model in the lecture.

signature_moments.moment: No agency can inspire freedom; total agency can enslave.
signature_moments.source_phrase: you have complete control over your life. It enslaves us.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It captures the paradox behind his critique of modern individualism.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Flourishing: living life to the best of one's ability under conditions of fate and limited control.
A divine excess that gods can get away with but humans cannot.

uncertainty_notes.note: The transcript renders Euripides as 'Euboides'; public prose should normalize only if necessary.
